summary:
The Carolingian Renaissance (c. 780–850 AD) was the first major cultural revival in medieval Western Europe — a deliberate program of educational, ecclesiastical, and artistic reform undertaken at the court of [[event:2138|Charlemagne]] and his successors, which preserved and transmitted Latin classical learning through the early medieval period and created the intellectual foundations for later European scholarship. Charlemagne, himself barely literate (he could read but reportedly never mastered writing), recognized education as essential for effective governance and Christian reform, and recruited the finest scholars of Europe to his court: the English scholar Alcuin of York, the Lombard historian Paul the Deacon, and the Visigoth Theodulf of Orleans.

The Carolingian Renaissance's most enduring contributions were textual and material: scholars at Charlemagne's court and the monasteries he reformed copied classical texts that would otherwise likely have been lost, creating the manuscript tradition on which medieval and Renaissance scholarship depended. The development of Carolingian minuscule script — clear, legible lowercase letters that replaced the cramped, varied scripts of the early medieval period — made manuscripts easier to read and copy, and is the direct ancestor of modern lowercase Latin letters. Educational reform standardized the curriculum around the seven liberal arts (trivium and quadrivium). Ecclesiastical reform improved the training of clergy and the quality of liturgical practice. Though modest compared to later renaissances, the Carolingian Renaissance was the essential intellectual bridge between antiquity and the 12th-century Renaissance.
